name: Harpe
description: The Harpe is a sickle-sword, a unique and instantly recognizable weapon from Greek mythology. Most famously wielded by Perseus, it is distinguished by its curved blade, often described as having a scythe-like or hooked shape. The Harpe's primary function was to sever, and its most celebrated use was when Perseus employed it to decapitate the Gorgon Medusa. The blade's sharp edge and inward curve allowed for a swift and decisive cut, effectively neutralizing Medusa's petrifying gaze. Beyond its physical properties, the Harpe carries symbolic weight, representing cunning, decisive action, and the ability to overcome seemingly insurmountable odds. It embodies the triumph of a hero against monstrous forces and the power of ingenuity in the face of danger. The Harpe’s legacy extends beyond the Medusa myth, appearing in other accounts as a symbol of divine favor and heroic prowess. Its unusual design and association with a pivotal moment in Greek mythology solidify its place as a significant and memorable artifact. The Harpe is not merely a weapon; it is a testament to the hero's journey, the struggle against evil, and the ultimate victory of good.
subtitle: The sickle-sword of heroes
origin: Gifted to Perseus, possibly from Hermes or Athena
currentLocation: Unknown, possibly lost or returned to the gods

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Medusa
keyMyths.description: Perseus used the Harpe to behead Medusa, relying on its curved blade to swiftly sever her head while avoiding her gaze. This act allowed him to defeat the Gorgon and acquire her head.

keyMyths.title: Cronus and Uranus
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, Cronus used a similar sickle, possibly the Harpe, to castrate Uranus, his father, leading to the birth of Aphrodite and the reign of the Titans. This marked the beginning of a new cosmic order.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing details on Perseus's quest and the use of the Harpe.
sources.text: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing details on Perseus's quest and the use of the Harpe.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: An important source for the creation myth and the story of Cronus and Uranus, potentially referencing the Harpe or a similar weapon.
